chunk_summary: This section discusses uncooked cornstarch regimens and sick-day management in glycogen storage disease type I (GSD I), focusing on preventing hypoglycemia through scheduled carbohydrate delivery, individualized dosing, and emergency protocols during illness.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of uncooked cornstarch in managing glycogen storage disease type I (GSD I).
Learn how to individualize cornstarch dosing based on age, tolerance, and glucose monitoring.
Recognize sick-day escalation strategies to prevent hypoglycemia and metabolic decompensation.
Identify long-term monitoring parameters linking dietary management to clinical outcomes.
key_facts: Uncooked cornstarch is dosed every 3–4 hours during the day in older infants and children to maintain safe glucose levels.
Overnight strategies include continuous enteral feeding or extended-release starch products in older children.
Sick-day escalation involves increasing carbohydrate frequency and density, with urgent IV dextrose for vomiting or lethargy.
Families should have a written emergency regimen and letter for illness management.
Long-term monitoring includes growth trajectory, hepatomegaly symptoms, fasting lipids, and uric acid levels.
reference_quote: Management is built around scheduled carbohydrate delivery across 24 hours using frequent meals plus uncooked cornstarch for slow glucose release, with continuous overnight feeds in infants and selected young children. (Source: Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Uncooked cornstarch regimens in GSD I
Prevention of hypoglycemia and metabolic complications
